title: Python Data and Functions
description: Build practical Python skills with lists, tuples, dictionaries, functions, helper modules, files, exceptions, and simple data cleaning.
subtitle: Organize data, write reusable code, and process files
intendedFor: Learners who know basic Python syntax and want to work with collections, functions, files, exceptions, and simple data-cleaning tasks.

courseOverview.recommendedSequence: Complete modules in order. This course builds from collections, to functions, to file-based data processing.
courseOverview.summary: This course teaches learners how to organize multiple values, write reusable functions, read and write files, handle exceptions, and clean simple data.

moduleSummary.title: Lists, Tuples, and Dictionaries
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Teach learners how to organize multiple values and choose the right collection type.
moduleSummary.outputFocus: Build a small contact book or inventory tracker using lists and dictionaries.

moduleSummary.title: Functions and Modularity
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Help learners organize code into reusable functions with clear inputs and outputs.
moduleSummary.outputFocus: Refactor a calculator or text utility into several reusable functions.

moduleSummary.title: Files, Exceptions, and Data Cleaning
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Teach learners how to read/write files and handle messy data without crashing programs.
moduleSummary.outputFocus: Build a small log or CSV cleaner that reads a file, validates rows, and writes a summary.

authoringGuidance: This course must always use profileId python, subjectSlug python, and courseSlug python-data-functions.
Do not use SQL datasets, SQL tables, sql_query recipes, datasetId, fixedSqlDialect, resultShape, or checkSql in Python lessons.
Module 5 should mostly use fixed_tests for deterministic collection behavior unless the exercise is explicitly function-return based.
Module 6 should prefer semantic checks for function structure, parameters, return behavior, print-vs-return, local scope, helper functions, and import/helper-module behavior.
Module 6 must explicitly teach import statements and helper files in using-imports-and-helper-files before Module 7 assumes import csv.
Module 7 should use fixed_tests with provided files / tests[].files fixtures and keep browser-python-files-runner behavior for file I/O lessons.
Module 7 should teach file creation as opening a path with write/append mode and writing content, not as manual workspace-file creation unless workspaceExpectations validates the required path.
Use folders/files in Module 6 only when modularity, helper files, or import organization is the learning goal.
Prefer provided starterFiles over asking learners to create files from scratch on the first compile pass.
Write code_input solutions as complete runnable programs for fixed_tests or clean function/class definitions for semantic exercises.
For function-return tasks, do not expose hidden harness code such as import ast, _parse_arg, _inputs, or ast.literal_eval in starterCode or solutionCode.
Keep examples focused on lists, dictionaries, functions, imports, helper modules, files, exceptions, and simple data cleaning.

notes: Function basics should usually be single-file main.py exercises.
Use semantic checks when the learner must define a function, use parameters, return a value, avoid print-only solutions, or demonstrate local scope.
Use semantic function_returns checks for function-return exercises. Do not convert list/dict/function arguments through visible stdin parsing wrappers.
Use fixed_tests only when the learner is practicing input(), print(), exact terminal output, file reading, file writing, paths, exceptions, CSV scripts, or other runnable-program behavior.
Teach imports explicitly in using-imports-and-helper-files before any lesson expects learners to understand import helpers, from helpers import ..., or import csv.
Use provided starterFiles for small modularity exercises, for example main.py plus helpers.py, or src/main.py plus src/helpers/formatting.py.
Do not ask learners to create new folders/files in normal graded exercises unless workspaceExpectations or equivalent required-path validation is available.
If the exercise needs a helper module but the checker cannot validate newly created files, provide the helper file in starterFiles and ask the learner to edit it.
If asking learners to create folders/files in a project-style exercise, state the exact required paths and validate them.
For writing-text-files, teach file creation through with open(path, "w") as file: file.write(...), then verify behavior through stdout summaries or validated fixture/output checks.
For simple-csv-processing, show import csv explicitly and prefer csv.DictReader examples that print selected fields or print dict(row) when dictionary display is required across Python versions.

Compile in this order: creating-and-indexing-lists; list-methods-and-mutation; looping-through-lists; dictionary-basics; updating-and-looping-dictionaries; nested-data-structures; defining-and-calling-functions; parameters-and-return-values; print-vs-return; scope-and-local-variables; decomposition-and-refactoring; using-imports-and-helper-files; docstrings-and-function-contracts; reading-text-files; writing-text-files; working-with-paths; try-except-basics; validating-and-cleaning-input; simple-csv-processing.
Use creating-and-indexing-lists as the first smoke topic.
Use reading-text-files as the first file/folder runtime proof topic.

assessmentAndDelivery.suggestedBeginnerRhythm: Two or three short lessons per week, with one module project after each module.
toolingSuggestions: Browser runner or local VS Code
Simple test runner for function-based exercises
assessmentAndDelivery.closingNote: Learners finish able to organize data, write reusable functions, and build small file-processing utilities.

notes: Use multi-file starter workspaces only for modularity/refactoring/import lessons where organization is the learning goal.
using-imports-and-helper-files must introduce import helpers and from helpers import clean_name style examples before Module 7 uses import csv.
Function-return exercises in this module should use semantic function_returns checks, not fixed stdout wrappers.
This module uses a browser files panel with provided fixture files for file I/O lessons.
Teach file creation through write/append modes and file.write(), with tests that verify observable behavior.
Use fixed_tests with tests[].files for reading/CSV/path lessons and avoid requiring manual file creation unless workspaceExpectations validates the required paths.
Prefer fixed_tests for direct list scripts; use semantic if the learner is completing a function that returns a list value.
Prefer fixed_tests for direct mutation scripts; use semantic when checking a function return value or mutated argument behavior.
Prefer fixed_tests for printed loop output; use semantic when the task is to return a derived list.
Prefer fixed_tests for direct dictionary scripts; use semantic when the learner completes a function that returns or updates dictionary data.
Prefer fixed_tests for printed dictionary loops; use semantic for dictionary transformation helper functions.
Prefer fixed_tests for observable nested-data scripts.
Use semantic checks when you need to verify returned nested data rather than output.
Prefer semantic checks for function definition and calling structure.
Keep this as a single-file main.py exercise unless modularity is the actual lesson goal.
Prefer semantic function_returns checks for parameters and return behavior.
Do not use visible _parse_arg / ast.literal_eval wrappers for list or dictionary arguments.
Prefer semantic checks that require return behavior and reject print-only solutions.
Prefer semantic checks for local scope behavior.
Use semantic checks for helper-function behavior and fixed_tests only for final runnable-program output.
If multiple files help teach modularity, provide them in starterFiles instead of asking learners to create them from scratch.
Teach import helpers, from helpers import ..., and simple helper-module organization.
Use starterFiles such as main.py and helpers.py so learners focus on imports rather than manual file creation.
Use semantic checks for helper function return behavior and fixed_tests for full main.py output behavior.
This topic must come before simple-csv-processing assumes import csv.
Prefer semantic or static checks for docstrings and function contracts.
Use fixed_tests with provided files / tests[].files fixtures.
Do not require learners to create input files manually.
Use fixed_tests with provided files / tests[].files fixtures where needed.
Teach file creation through open(path, "w") or open(path, "a") and file.write().
Check stdout summaries unless output-file assertions already cover the behavior.
If asking learners to create a specific output file path, use workspaceExpectations or an output-file assertion path supported by the runner.
Use fixed_tests with nested fixture paths such as data/input.txt.
Teach relative paths only; avoid absolute paths and OS-specific locations.
Use fixed_tests and add an optional semantic check for try/except structure only if the checker supports it.
Use semantic checks when learners write cleaning helper functions that return cleaned data.
Use fixed_tests when the lesson is a runnable cleaning script that reads input or files and prints a report.
Use fixed_tests with provided CSV fixture files.
Explicitly include import csv in explanation, starter code, or solution because imports are taught in using-imports-and-helper-files.
Use csv.DictReader for dictionary rows and prefer printing selected fields. When a full row dictionary must be printed, use print(dict(row)) for Python-version-safe output.

modules.title: Lists, Tuples, and Dictionaries
modules.purpose: Teach learners how to organize multiple values and choose the right collection type.
learningObjectives: Use tuples for fixed grouped values
modules.moduleProject: Build a small contact book or inventory tracker using lists and dictionaries.

topics.title: Creating and Indexing Lists
topics.summary: Create lists and access items by position.

topics.title: List Methods and Mutation
topics.summary: Use append, remove, pop, sort, and understand mutation.

topics.title: Looping Through Lists
topics.summary: Process each list item and build derived results.

topics.title: Dictionary Basics
topics.summary: Store and retrieve values using keys.

topics.title: Updating and Looping Dictionaries
topics.summary: Add, update, remove, and loop through dictionary data.

topics.title: Nested Data Structures
topics.summary: Represent real-world data with lists of dictionaries and nested containers.

modules.title: Functions and Modularity
modules.purpose: Help learners organize code into reusable functions, helper modules, and simple imports with clear inputs and outputs.
learningObjectives: Separate program logic into small pieces
Use simple imports and helper modules
guidedExercises: Import helper functions from another file
modules.moduleProject: Refactor a calculator or text utility into reusable functions, then optionally split safe helper logic into main.py and helpers.py.

topics.title: Defining and Calling Functions
topics.summary: Create functions and call them with arguments.

topics.title: Parameters and Return Values
topics.summary: Pass data into functions and return results.

topics.title: Print vs Return
topics.summary: Understand when to display output and when to return data.

topics.title: Scope and Local Variables
topics.summary: Understand where variables exist and why local scope matters.

topics.title: Decomposition and Refactoring
topics.summary: Break a larger task into clear helper functions.

topics.title: Using Imports and Helper Files
topics.summary: Use import statements and small helper modules to organize reusable code.
learningGoals: Import helper functions from another file
Explain when helper modules make code easier to organize

topics.title: Docstrings and Function Contracts
topics.summary: Document what a function expects and returns.

modules.title: Files, Exceptions, and Data Cleaning
modules.purpose: Teach learners how to read files, create/write files, import standard-library data tools, and handle messy data without crashing programs.
learningObjectives: Use the csv module for simple CSV processing
modules.moduleProject: Build a small log or CSV cleaner that reads a file, validates rows, and writes a summary file.

topics.title: Reading Text Files
topics.summary: Open files, read text, and close resources safely.

topics.title: Writing Text Files
topics.summary: Create or overwrite text files by opening paths in write/append mode and writing content.

topics.title: Working With Paths
topics.summary: Understand relative paths and simple file organization.

topics.title: Try/Except Basics
topics.summary: Catch expected errors and keep programs usable.

topics.title: Validating and Cleaning Input
topics.summary: Clean text, check missing values, and reject invalid rows.

topics.title: Simple CSV Processing
topics.summary: Use the csv module to read comma-separated rows and transform them into dictionaries.
